summary: Smoky Dark Star & soaring Lovelight jam fuel high-energy show
blurb: Dark Star leads a blazing second set that segues into an electrifying Turn On Your Lovelight suite. The first set shines with tight renditions of Bertha, Cumberland Blues and Brokedown Palace, while Keith’s piano dominates the mix. Overall the concert delivers high‑energy playing and memorable jams.
review: December 15, 1971 at Ann Arbor’s Hill Auditorium captured a Grateful Dead that was firing on all cylinders. The first set rolls out with crisp, punchy performances of classics – Bertha roars, Cumberland Blues grooves tight, and Brokedown Palace glides with an almost reverent calm before the band launches into a rollicking Run Rudolph Run. By the time Dark Star erupts in the second set, Jerry’s lead guitar is “on fire,” soaring over Keith Godchaux’s piano that sits front‑and‑center, hot and unapologetically bold. The Dark Star segues into a marathon Turn On Your Lovelight suite (Turn On Your Lovelight → I’m A King Bee → Mannish Boy (I’m A Man) → Turn On Your Lovelight) that feels like an hour‑long journey of pure pleasure, with Bill Kreutzmann’s drums locked in and Phil’s bass providing a solid, if sometimes recessed, foundation. The encore caps it off with One More Saturday Night and Uncle John’s Band, leaving the audience buzzing long after the lights came up.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All six recordings are marked positive, with multiple reviewers calling the show "stellar," "high‑energy," and highlighting the same standout moments (Dark Star, Lovelight suite, Bertha, Brokedown Palace). Consistent praise across sources warrants a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Offers the clearest, most balanced soundboard mix with superb clarity and only minor left‑channel buzz; captures the full show without dropouts.

key_highlights: Dark Star serves as a menacing, soaring centerpiece of the second set.
Turn On Your Lovelight suite (including King Bee and Mannish Boy) delivers an hour‑long jam that’s consistently praised.
Keith Godchaux’s piano/organ is hot and prominent, adding a distinctive character to the performance.
First‑set tightness shines on Bertha, Cumberland Blues, and Brokedown Palace.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s lead work is described as soaring, on fire and expressive throughout, especially during Dark Star and the Lovelight suite.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass provides a solid foundation; though sometimes buried in the mix, his playing remains reliable when audible.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocals are noted as terrific and clear, cutting through the occasional mix issues.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming is praised for its firm footing and driving energy, with cymbals sitting well in the overall balance.
